title: Add semantic consistency check to skill audit
description: The current `soul_manager.audit_skill()` checks for known-bad patterns (eval, sudo, base64 payloads, etc.). This is pattern matching — it catches known fingerprints but misses the Axios-class attack: a skill that uses *legitimate* operations (real HTTP requests, real file writes, real publish APIs) in ways that don't match its stated purpose. Add a semantic consistency check: when a skill uses powerful-but-legitimate operations (external network calls, subprocess, file system writes outside MIRA_ROOT), verify that those operations are coherent with the skill's declared title and description. A skill titled 'Format essay paragraphs' that makes HTTP POST requests to an external URL is a semantic mismatch, even if no individual line triggers the pattern blocklist.
rationale: The reading note's core distinction: 'detecting known malicious payloads is pattern matching; judging whether a legitimate permission is being abused is semantic judgment.' The Axios attacker used a real publish pipeline — no anomalous signals, only wrong semantics. Mira's audit is currently all pattern matching (suspicious network requests, dangerous ops, obfuscated code). A skill imported from an external source could pass all four checks while using legitimate `requests.post()` to exfiltrate data, because the call itself is not suspicious — only its relationship to the skill's stated purpose is. The audit needs a layer that asks: does this skill's behavior match its declared intent?
diff_description: In `soul_manager.audit_skill()`, after the existing four checks, add a fifth check: `_check_semantic_consistency(skill_title, skill_description, skill_code)`. This function uses a local LLM call (oMLX, same as the `secret` agent — no cloud) to compare the skill's declared purpose against the set of operations it actually performs. Extract the operation categories from the code (network calls, subprocess, file I/O outside sandbox, API key usage) and ask: 'Given that this skill claims to do X, is there a plausible legitimate reason for it to perform these operations?' Return a boolean + explanation. If the check returns False, block the skill and log the mismatch reason. This is not a replacement for the existing pattern checks — it runs after them as an additional layer specifically targeting legitimate-feature abuse.
